user_input: During my interactive session with RStudio, I noticed that if I am not active on the page while my model is running, I am logged out of the RStudio server, but the login I use for the HPC does not work to log back into the RStudio server. I have attempted to refresh the HPC main page, log out and back in, and then relaunch my RStudio session that is currently running. However, when I try to relaunch it, I am redirected to an RStudio login page that displays the message: “Temporary server error, please try again.”  When I attempt to log in using the same credentials I use for the HPC, it does not allow me to log back in. I have also tried Chrome and Safari, and seem to run into the same login page. My models still run, but I would like to be able to access the R page while they are running so I can see its progress. Thank you!
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

'text': 'IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-start.png)

Fill the job details, then press the button `Launch` at the bottom of the page. This will lead you to your job page.
When the job is starting, the page should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-connect.png)

Press the button `Connect to RStudio Server`. This will open the RStudio IDE in a new browser tab, which should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-firsttime.png)

The IDE is made of panels and tabs. From the default tab `Console`, 3 panels are visible:

* The actual console, to the left, with a prompt to an interactive R session.
* A first management panel, on the top-right corner, to manage Environments, check your session history or the available tutorials.
* A second management panel, on the bottom-right corner, to manage files, plots, packages and look at plots.

#### Managing user environments and projects on RStudio[¶](#managing-user-environments-and-projects-on-rstudio "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is a complete IDE that allows direct project creation and management.

To create a new project, select `File` from the top toolbar, then `New project..`, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-newproject.png)

then follows the instructions from the pop-up wizard. It is good practice to create a new folder
for each new project. At the end, a setup file ending with *.Rproj* is created.

Use the console to add new packages to your project, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-session.png)

## Frequently asked questions (FAQ)[¶](#frequently-asked-questions-faq "Permalink to this headline")

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.
* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

  > + Please prefer to use the RDS gateway *rds.uis.cam.ac.uk* for file transfers. This will request TOTP less frequently.
* I don’t have a smart phone on which I can install an OATH-compliant TOTP app such as Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy or FreeOTP.

  > + The proper solution for you is to ask your department to purchase an inexpensive OATH TOTP compatible token device.
  > + If you already have such a device and use it for login to another service, you will need a new device for use with CSD3 unless it supports multiple accounts.
  > + Any device needs to support **6 digit, SHA1** OATH TOTP with **30 seconds time interval**, which non-programmable devices may well not do. In particular, please note that the *SafeID/Classic* model provided by the UIS for Microsoft services **is known not to work** with the Google style of TOTP used on CSD3.
  > + [YubiKeys](https://www.yubico.com/) (used in conjunction with an application on phone, desktop or laptop) are known to work (although we have not been able to test all combinations of device and platform).
  > + Combinations of hardware token and platform which we have tested and are known to work are listed below (please note that these are not recommendations, merely examples which we have made to work):

#### Adding virtual environments[¶](#adding-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To use your own virtual environments in the Jupyter Notebook app, you need to add them externally. Use the following steps to do so:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. If needed, create a new virtual environment using the commands described in the section [Setup Jupyter on CSD3](../software-packages/jupyter.html#setup-jupyter-on-csd3)
3. **Activate the virtual environment** (otherwise, your installed packages won’t be registered)
4. Deploy the python module **ipykernel** with the command line:

   ```
   pip install ipykernel
   ```
5. Add your virtual environment to the Jupyter Notebook as new kernel, with the command line:

   ```
   ipython kernel install --user --name=<virtual environment name>
   ```
6. Logout.
7. Start or refresh your jupyter notebook session, as described in the previous section [Accessing through Login-Web](loginweb-apps-jupyter.html#jupyter-access-loginweb)
8. Start a new logbook by clicking on the button “New” on the right-hand side:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-envs.jpg)

Your virtual environment should be listed under the selected `Python3` as shown in the above illustration. Selecting the virtual environment as kernel will give you access to the python packages you have installed in the virtual environment.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-with-virtual-env.png)

#### Delisting virtual environments[¶](#delisting-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To remove unwanted kernels from the Jupyter notebook:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. At the prompt:

   ```
   module load jupyterlab/3
   jupyter kernelspec list
   ```
3. The last command should give you the same list of kernels as the jupyter notebook app. Select the kernel to be deleted, then type:

   ```
   jupyter kernelspec uninstall <myenv>
   ```

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler versions being too old, then try loading a more recent compiler before starting R. If you run into problems that refer to the C or C++ standard versions then try adding:

```
CFLAGS = -std=c99
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11
```

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

user_input:
Dear support team
I am having trouble accessing the RFS service for my lab group (TP group, Condensed Matter Physics, Maxwell Centre). It has already been mapped, and it was working fine, but today it asked me for the password of my username (of the computer I was using). As you know, those are generated tokens. So, I generated a new one for the computer I am using, but it does not accept it. How can we fix that? It is urgent that I have access to my data.
Best regards
Patricia N. Lazo Paravicini
Pilizota Lab
Department of Physics
University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RFS Access / Credential Confusion
Technical Action Required: No (workflow clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to use a generated token/device password for RFS access on Windows. The correct fix is to ensure they are on the Cambridge VPN or network, follow the Windows RFS instructions, and use their normal UIS credentials for Access Blue rather than the VPN/device token.

ACTION
1) Confirm the user is on the Cambridge VPN or directly on the network.
2) Direct them to the Windows RFS guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html
3) Clarify that the username/password prompt is for normal UIS credentials, not the VPN/device token.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patricia,

Please make sure you are connected to the Cambridge VPN or the Cambridge network directly, and follow the Windows guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html

For this login prompt, you should use your normal UIS credentials rather than the generated VPN/device token.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / MFA [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with accessing the RFS service due to MFA-related problems [1]. The user has already generated a new token, but it is not being accepted [1]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, the user should try deleting the account in their Authenticator and recreating it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*) [1]. If this does not work, the user should contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request a reset of the MFA secret [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you are using the correct QR code and secret key to set up your MFA [1]?
- Have you tried setting **export LANG=en_GB.UTF-8** in your Mac terminal before performing the ssh [1]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the MFA issue, please try deleting the account in your Authenticator and recreating it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type [1]. If this does not work, please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request a reset of the MFA secret [1]. 
Please get back in touch if any portal step fails [1].

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not attempt to guess or bypass MFA setup [1].
- Always use the correct QR code and secret key to set up MFA [1].

REFERENCE KEY

## Frequently asked questions (FAQ)[¶](#frequently-asked-questions-faq "Permalink to this headline")

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.
* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

  > + Please prefer to use the RDS gateway *rds.uis.cam.ac.uk* for file transfers. This will request TOTP less frequently.
* I don’t have a smart phone on which I can install an OATH-compliant TOTP app such as Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy or FreeOTP.

  > + The proper solution for you is to ask your department to purchase an inexpensive OATH TOTP compatible token device.
  > + If you already have such a device and use it for login to another service, you will need a new device for use with CSD3 unless it supports multiple accounts.
  > + Any device needs to support **6 digit, SHA1** OATH TOTP with **30 seconds time interval**, which non-programmable devices may well not do. In particular, please note that the *SafeID/Classic* model provided by the UIS for Microsoft services **is known not to work** with the Google style of TOTP used on CSD3.
  > + [YubiKeys](https://www.yubico.com/) (used in conjunction with an application on phone, desktop or laptop) are known to work (although we have not been able to test all combinations of device and platform).

'text': '# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. ## PI Accessing Portal[¶](#pi-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (IdP of last resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### **University Login (MyAccessID)**

Step 1: Click the “MyAccessID” button to authenticate using your university credentials.

[![Access ID Login Option](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)

If this is your first time accessing MyAccessID, please ensure that you provide the necessary details and take a moment to review their policy. If you have further issues with using MyAccessID and you have a university email address, please contact your local IT department to ensure you are able to use MyAccessID.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/access_id_registration.png)](../_images/access_id_registration.png)

To Whom It May Concern,
For context, I have been using the Remote Desktop interactive sessions via the web interface for about a year.  As of this morning, requesting a desktop no longer provides the “Launch Remote Desktop” button.  Please see the attached screenshot. I expected to see the “Launch Remote Desktop” button as shown in the second screenshot. I would appreciate any insights or support. Thank you.
-Pete
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / Remote Desktop Launch Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The missing “Launch Remote Desktop” button can be caused by launch-form settings such as project or account selection, but the screenshots are needed to confirm the exact cause. The safest next step is to review the full launch settings and confirm which project or account is selected.

ACTION
1) Ask the user for a full screenshot of the launch settings if not already available.
2) Confirm that the correct project or account is selected in the Remote Desktop form.
3) If project selection is wrong, explain that the default project may not be appropriate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Pete,

Please send us the full launch settings for the Remote Desktop session, including the selected project/account, so that we can confirm what is preventing the expected button from appearing.

If the current screenshot does not show the full launch form, please send one that includes the relevant project/account fields.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Remote Desktop / Login-Web
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is unable to see the "Launch Remote Desktop" button when requesting a desktop via the web interface [1][2][3]. This suggests a potential issue with the Login-Web interface or the user's account settings.

ACTION
Check the user's account settings and Login-Web configuration to ensure that the Remote Desktop option is enabled [1][2]. If the issue persists, try clearing the browser cache and cookies, and then attempt to launch the Remote Desktop again [3]. If the problem continues, escalate the issue to the HPC support team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide a screenshot of the exact error message or the page where the "Launch Remote Desktop" button is missing?
- Have you tried accessing the Remote Desktop from a different browser or device?
- Are there any other issues or errors when using the Login-Web interface?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We are currently investigating the issue with the missing "Launch Remote Desktop" button. In the meantime, please try clearing your browser cache and cookies, and then attempt to launch the Remote Desktop again. If the issue persists, please provide a screenshot of the error message or the page where the button is missing.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that all interactions with the Login-Web interface are conducted over a secure connection (https) [1][3].
- Be cautious when sharing screenshots or other sensitive information, and redact any confidential data [2].

[3] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html::chunk::0000

Once the desktop is ready to be used, your session manager will show a job which looks like the image below:

![XFCE Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-xfce.png)

The remaining options shown under `Interactive Apps` will use the same functionality to launch specific applications, rather than full desktops.

### MATLAB[¶](#matlab "Permalink to this headline")

MATLAB allows matrix manipulations, plotting of functions and data, implementation of algorithms, creation of user interfaces, and interfacing with programs written in other languages, including C, C++, C#, Java, Fortran and Python.

#### Accessing through Login-Web[¶](#accessing-through-login-web "Permalink to this headline")

All the desktop apps can be found within the `Interactive Apps` drop-down in the Login-Web web portal as shown in the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-menu.png)

After selecting MATLAB, you will arrive at this page:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-select.png)

Here you can select the resources you would like your MATLAB session to have using the menu on the right hand side of the page. If you’d like to receive an email notifying you of when this has happened, please select `I would like to receive an email when the session starts`. After clicking `Launch`, a new session will be queued. Once the session is active your page will look like the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-launch.png)

To access the interface you can choose from `Launch MATLAB` to reach the application as shown below and use it normally, or `View Only (Shareable Link)` to share the application interface with colleagues in `View Only` mode.

#### Using MATLAB[¶](#using-matlab "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have entered your session, you should see a new tab. Allow MATLAB a moment to start up, then your screen should eventually look like the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab.png)

This is a typical MATLAB interface that should be familiar:

* Current Folder — Access your files.
* Command Window — Enter commands at the command line, indicated by the prompt (>>).
* Workspace — Explore data that you create or import from files.

The desktop also includes these applications:
:   * Web Browser
    * Terminal Emulator
    * File Manager

**Further Reading**: [MathWorks support page](https://www.mathworks.com/support/learn-with-matlab-tutorials.html)

### Jupyter Notebook[¶](#jupyter-notebook "Permalink to this headline")

The Jupyter Notebook is an open-source web application that allows you to create and share documents that contain live code, equations, visualisations and narrative text. Uses include: data cleaning and transformation, numerical simulation, statistical modeling, data visualisation, machine learning, and much more.

#### Accessing through Login-Web[¶](#jupyter-access-loginweb "Permalink to this headline")

All the desktop apps can be found within the `Interactive Apps` dropdown in the Login-Web web portal. After selecting Jupyter, you will arrive at this page:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-menu.png)

4. At this point, you can submit the job using the green `Submit` button from the `Jobs` page; or return to this job later to modify it further. You can also monitor the job status here. Currently you must reload the page to see job status changes.

### Job Management[¶](#id1 "Permalink to this headline")

On the main screen for `Jobs`, click on a job to examine it.

#### Submit Job[¶](#submit-job "Permalink to this headline")

If the job has not been submitted, the `Green Submit` button will be active, and will submit the selected job to the queue.

#### Stop Job[¶](#stop-job "Permalink to this headline")

The `Stop` button will allow you to kill a running job.

#### Delete Job[¶](#delete-job "Permalink to this headline")

The `Delete` button will allow you to delete a job.

### Active Jobs[¶](#active-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

The `Active Jobs` application will show all of your jobs currently in the queue (running or queued), regardless of how the jobs were submitted. You can click the button at the top to switch from:

> * All jobs submitted from your CSD3 user account
> * All jobs submitted by all CSD3 users

You can also use the `Filter` tool to search jobs based on system, status (running, queued, or held), CSD3 user account, etc.

![Jobs menu open](../_images/ondemand-jobs-listing.png)

### Job Inspection[¶](#job-inspection "Permalink to this headline")

Clicking the arrow to the left of each job will show more information about the job.

![Jobs inspection open](../_images/ondemand-jobs-inspection.png)

## Shell Access[¶](#shell-access "Permalink to this headline")

Login-Web provides the ability to SSH to a CSD3 login node from inside your web browser. To obtain a terminal, select the `Clusters` menu and choose the desired flavour of login node.

![Shell menu open](../_images/ondemand-shell-menu.png)

Note

Please note that this action will open a “pop-up” window. If your browser is configured to block pop-ups, you will not see the terminal.

## Interactive Apps[¶](#interactive-apps "Permalink to this headline")

![Apps menu open](../_images/ondemand-apps-menu.png)

This function launches a browser-based remote Linux desktop on which to run GUI applications, without the hassle or performance problems associated with X11 forwarding or custom VNC clients. To use this feature, please select an item from under Desktops, GUIs or Servers from the `Interactive Apps` menu.

### Remote Desktop[¶](#remote-desktop "Permalink to this headline")

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-select.png)

This option will launch a full remote desktop in a browser tab. Select `Remote Desktop` and you will arrive on the page shown below. Click `Launch`. You may need to wait for a few minutes for the application to launch.

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-wait.png)

To access the desktop you can choose from `Launch Remote Desktop` to reach your desktop as shown below and interact with it normally, or `View Only (Shareable Link)` to share your desktop with colleagues in `View Only` mode.

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-starting.png)

Login-Web is a “one stop shop” for access to our High Performance Computing resources. With Login-Web, it is possible to upload and download files, create, edit, submit, run GUI applications, and connect to nodes via SSH, all via a web browser, with no client software to install and configure.

## System Requirements[¶](#system-requirements "Permalink to this headline")

Currently, the site is confirmed to work with newer versions of Chrome (22+), Firefox (16+), and Microsoft Edge (11+). We are still working to expand availability to additional clients, and are planning on including compatibility for mobile devices (phones and tablets) in the future.

Note

When logging in for the first time to Login-Web, you will be required to set up MFA, please see the MFA documentation for more information: <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html>

If you need to reconfigure your MFA please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk and request a token reset.

### Connecting[¶](#connecting "Permalink to this headline")

To connect to Login-Web, visit <https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk>. The first page of the Login-Web site redirects you to the login page.

Tip

For a video guide on how to use Login-Web for CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

### Logging in[¶](#logging-in "Permalink to this headline")

Login with your CSD3 HPC credentials: CRSid and password.

![Login page](../_images/ondemand-login.png)

Once completed you will be presented with this page:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page.png)

Or this page if your browser window is more compact:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page-compact.png)

The three-lined icon to the right of the compact page expands when clicked to show the full toolbar available on the non-compact page. At this point, it is possible to access the tools available in Login-Web.

Login-Web provides a web-based File Explorer that can be used to upload and download files to/from your CSD3 home directory or project directory, and copy, delete, rename, and edit files. Below you can see a screenshot of the main interface:

Important

The graphical visualisation nodes *gpu-e-1,…, gpu-e-7* retired on Monday 10th July 2023 and were replaced by new NVIDIA L40 nodes *gpu-r-1,…, gpu-r-10*.

## Starting a new VNC session[¶](#starting-a-new-vnc-session "Permalink to this headline")

The recommended form of remote desktop uses the freely downloadable software [TurboVNC](http://www.turbovnc.org/). This is because TurboVNC, which is centrally installed on CSD3, contains an improved codec for better transmission of rapidly varying 3D images. Hardware accelerated 3D (see below) will still work with other forms of VNC (or e.g. [X2Go](https://wiki.x2go.org/)), although in these cases the performance may be decreased depending on the codecs available for compressing and decompressing the desktop images.

Since 3D visualisation-capable graphics cards are only attached to a subset of nodes, in order to use 3D the TurboVNC server should be started on one of these nodes. Please see the [startgfx](#startgfx) section for instructions for launching and managing these specialised remote desktops, however the general descriptions of first-time password setting and the SSH-tunnel method of connecting to the remote desktop that follow remain applicable in the 3D case. You will probably wish to try creating a non-graphical remote desktop initially.

If you don’t plan to make use of 3D graphics, then you should launch a VNC session on an ordinary login node as follows.

To start a VNC session, first ssh/PuTTY in your usual way to a CSD3 login node. Obviously it is best to choose a relatively non-busy node (the commands top or w will display current system load). Then do:

```
vncserver
```

(where in the default environment this will refer to the correct version of VNC).

The first time you run this you will be asked to set a VNC password. Note that this is **not your login password** and should be set to something different (but still strong) - its purpose is to protect your remote desktop from access by other users who are already logged into the cluster. You will also be asked for a second, optional password which can be shared with others if you want to give someone else the ability to see, but not change, your remote desktop, e.g. to show them some simulation result - this need not be set. Once you have set a VNC password it will be stored in your home directory and you will not be asked to set it again (unless you delete the ~/.vnc/passwd file).

The vncserver command will start a VNC session on the next available display number. The name of the display will be reported by the vncserver command in the form hostname:N where N is the display number. You will need to note the display number, and also remember the hostname in order to connect to the correct desktop later.

Note that vncserver -list will list active sessions belonging to you which are running on the same node, and -kill can be used to terminate a specified display number (for the 3D desktops, the *showgfx* and *killgfx* special commands make this easy).

Hello,
I hope you're well.
I'm a current PhD student and was just made a Data user on the Research Data Store for the Genetics_HPC partition by a collaborator. Upon opening the authentication link, I completely mistakenly clicked "Do not agree", rather than "Agree". The latter was certainly my intention, and now cannot access the drive.
Would there be any way to reverse this and reauthenticate?
Thank you,
Maria
Maria Jelen<[https://www.mrc-cbu.cam.ac.uk/people/maria.jelen/](https://www.mrc-cbu.cam.ac.uk/people/maria.jelen/)> (she/her)
Second-Year PhD Student at 4D Lab<[https://www.astlelab.com/](https://www.astlelab.com/)>
Medical Research Council Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit
mbj26@<[maria.jelen@](mailto:maria.jelen@)>cam.ac.uk
[cid:478d2978-d5c5-41c0-97e4-1b8771985808]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Access / Invitation Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user accidentally rejected the storage access invitation by clicking “Do not agree”. Helpdesk cannot reverse that state directly; the storage/data manager who originally added the user must re-add or re-invite them.

ACTION
1) Explain that the original invitation cannot simply be toggled back by helpdesk.
2) Direct the user to the Data Manager or project owner who granted access.
3) Ask that person to re-add/reinvite the user to the storage.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Maria,

In this case the Data Manager who added you to the storage will need to re-add or re-invite you. We cannot reverse that response directly from the helpdesk side.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Access / Authentication [1][2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][2]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [1][2]
Urgency: Medium [1][2]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has clicked "Do not agree" instead of "Agree" on the authentication link for the Research Data Store (RDS) and is now unable to access the drive [1][2]. The correct process for accepting the invitation and terms and conditions is outlined in the documentation [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
The user should try to re-access the authentication link and click "Agree" to accept the terms and conditions [1][2]. If the issue persists, the user should contact the service desk for assistance [1][2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide a screenshot of the error message you are receiving?
- Have you tried clearing your browser cache and cookies before attempting to access the authentication link again?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Maria,
Thank you for reaching out to us about the issue with accessing the Research Data Store (RDS). To resolve this, please try to re-access the authentication link and click "Agree" to accept the terms and conditions [1][2]. If you are still experiencing issues, please contact the service desk for further assistance [1][2].
Best regards,
HPC Support

## PI Accessing Portal[¶](#pi-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (IdP of last resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### Accepting the Invitation[¶](#id1 "Permalink to this headline")

Upon entering the system, a pop-up notification will prompt you to accept the invitation. To continue, simply click on “Accept Invitation.”

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)

**Once accepted, users need to wait approximately 5 seconds for synchronization. A pop-up notification will appear at the top right of the screen confirming that the invitation has been accepted.**

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/accept_terms.png)](../_images/accept_terms.png)

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_user_projects.png)](../_images/waldur_user_projects.png)

Upon opening your project, kindly navigate to the Resources tab to review the available resources that you can access.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_user_resources.png)](../_images/waldur_user_resources.png)

On the main page, you will find the URL to access the system. Please click on it and begin using the platform after completing the authentication process.
Please note that you will gain an additional MFA code, labelled “CSD3:<userid>”, where <userid> is your Dawn username. Please do not delete this code.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_user_gettingstarted.png)](../_images/waldur_user_gettingstarted.png)

Upon completing the authentication process, you will gain access to the resources.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/hpc_weblogin.png)](../_images/hpc_weblogin.png)

## PI Managing Projects[¶](#pi-managing-projects "Permalink to this headline")

**Understanding roles and permissions for Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal:**

There are three roles you can have on a project:

* Principal Investigator
  :   + Can invite team members to the project to access the resources requested.
      + Can manage team members (add/remove) users from a project.
* Co-Investigator
  :   + Can invite team members to the project to access the resources requested.
      + Can manage team members (add/remove) users from a project.
* Researcher
  :   + Can access and utilize project resources.
      + Cannot create new resources or invite team members.

The Principal Investigator (PI) is a special role that should be held only by the person (or people) who are legally responsible for the project.

In doing so, the PI confirms that they have conducted the required due diligence on the person they are inviting, and that they are happy to take responsibility for the actions of the person they invite.

Dear HPC cam support,
I am a new user that has been added to the project and i already set up my
MFA auth with MyAccessID and can access the dashboard of the project.
I have linked my ssh public key through the dashboard, although the mail
that i received says to send the public also to this email address. Thus, i
attach it just in case is needed.
ssh-ed25519
AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIACYZwByA+c+iUfw7IQIy0u9my6yGu/FdXd1hnahrjYz
matteo.baldan.7@studenti.unipd.it
I got the message to "Select “Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop” and launch a
job on the pvc9 partition, requesting at least one GPU on one node, using
the above project.", but it seems that I am not able to find such
interactive apps and remote desktop..
I wanted to login with ssh access but I do not know which password would be
my credentials for the ssh access.
Thanks in advance, best regards,
Matteo
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN Onboarding / SSH Access / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has been added to a DAWN project but is unsure how to access the service and what credentials to use. The correct onboarding path is to confirm whether the submitted public key has been linked to the DAWN account, complete the separate SSH MFA setup, then connect using the corresponding private key and the DAWN login host.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the public key submitted via the dashboard/email has been linked to the DAWN account.
2) Direct the user to the SSH MFA guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk
3) Provide the DAWN login command:
   `ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
4) Clarify that the SSH client must point to the private key paired with the public key they supplied.
5) Share the DAWN/PVC documentation:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Matteo,

If your public key has already been linked to the DAWN account, the next step is to complete the separate SSH MFA setup and then connect using:
`ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

If your SSH client asks for authentication, make sure it is using the private key that matches the public key you sent us.

Best regards,
HPC Support

The user has set up MFA and has access to the dashboard but is having trouble finding the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop" and is unsure about the password for SSH access [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To access the CPU cluster nodes, the user should use the command: ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk [1][3]. 
The user should use their UIS password for the initial login [1]. 
For further assistance with finding the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop", the user can visit the Login-Web interface at https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)

- Have you tried accessing the system via the web interface (login-web) [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To access the CPU cluster nodes, please use the command: ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk [1][3]. 
You should use your UIS password for the initial login [1]. 
For further assistance with finding the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop", you can visit the Login-Web interface at https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk [4].
Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.

- It is essential to set up MFA to access the HPC services [1][2].
- The user should use their UIS password for the initial login, and it is possible to change the password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node [1].

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. CSD3 has several login nodes, for reliability and to share the interactive workload. Also there are specific login nodes associated with each generation of hardware. For reasons of security it is necessary to use SSH in order to connect - any Linux distribution will contain the *ssh* client which can be used for interactive logins, and also *scp*, *sftp* and *rsync* which employ SSH natively for file transfers. If your Linux/MacOSX/UNIX machine does not have ssh, firstly please check your distribution for it (ensuring you get the most recent version available). Recent versions of Microsoft Windows also include the OpenSSH ssh command-line client as an optional feature (alternatively, PuTTY is often used on Microsoft Windows).

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

To use the icelake CPU nodes in particular - also suitable for the production GPU cluster (ampere GPU nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

Note that you may need to add the -X option to the above ssh command in order to enable transparent forwarding of X applications to your local screen (this assumes that you have an X server running on your local machine). The OpenSSH version of ssh sometimes requires that you use -Y instead of -X (try -Y if applications don’t appear or die with errors). These options to ssh direct the X communication through an encrypted tunnel, and should ‘just work’. In the bad old days before SSH the highly insecure method of persuading X windows to display on remote screens involved commands such as xauth and xhost - you do not and never will need to use either of these with SSH, and if you try to use them you may open up everything you type (including passwords) to be read, and even changed, by evil-doers - so *do not* try these methods, they are both redundant and dangerous.

Similarly scp, sftp or rsync should also be accepted, incoming to CSD3. Of course doing the same in an outward direction should also work, provided the other system does not block SSH.

Login-Web is a “one stop shop” for access to our High Performance Computing resources. With Login-Web, it is possible to upload and download files, create, edit, submit, run GUI applications, and connect to nodes via SSH, all via a web browser, with no client software to install and configure.

## System Requirements[¶](#system-requirements "Permalink to this headline")

Currently, the site is confirmed to work with newer versions of Chrome (22+), Firefox (16+), and Microsoft Edge (11+). We are still working to expand availability to additional clients, and are planning on including compatibility for mobile devices (phones and tablets) in the future.

Note

When logging in for the first time to Login-Web, you will be required to set up MFA, please see the MFA documentation for more information: <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html>

If you need to reconfigure your MFA please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk and request a token reset.

### Connecting[¶](#connecting "Permalink to this headline")

To connect to Login-Web, visit <https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk>. The first page of the Login-Web site redirects you to the login page.

Tip

For a video guide on how to use Login-Web for CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

### Logging in[¶](#logging-in "Permalink to this headline")

Login with your CSD3 HPC credentials: CRSid and password.

![Login page](../_images/ondemand-login.png)

Once completed you will be presented with this page:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page.png)

Or this page if your browser window is more compact:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page-compact.png)

The three-lined icon to the right of the compact page expands when clicked to show the full toolbar available on the non-compact page. At this point, it is possible to access the tools available in Login-Web.

## File Transfer and Management[¶](#file-transfer-and-management "Permalink to this headline")

This function allows the transfer of data to and from [Research Storage Services](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store) project areas, or your CSD3 home directory. To use the file transfer client, select `Files` in the main menu.

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-menu.png)

Login-Web provides a web-based File Explorer that can be used to upload and download files to/from your CSD3 home directory or project directory, and copy, delete, rename, and edit files. Below you can see a screenshot of the main interface:

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files.png)

### Navigating[¶](#navigating "Permalink to this headline")

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-goto.png)

By default, the file browser will be looking at your home directory. If you have access to a RDS or RCS project space, browse to the rds or rcs subdirectories of your home directory as normal; alternatively if you know the full path to the directory you can move stepwise through the path near the top of the window. ### Accepting the Invitation[¶](#id1 "Permalink to this headline")

Upon entering the system, a pop-up notification will prompt you to accept the invitation. To continue, simply click on “Accept Invitation.”

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)

**Once accepted, users need to wait approximately 5 seconds for synchronization. A pop-up notification will appear at the top right of the screen confirming that the invitation has been accepted.**

Upon acceptance, you will be directed to the home page. The next step involves reading and agreeing to the terms and conditions.

Dawn has several policies that must be accepted before you can utilize any of its services. Once you log in, a list of these policies will be presented for your acceptance. You will encounter them on screens that resemble the following format.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/accept_terms.png)](../_images/accept_terms.png)

Upon accepting the policy, you will be online and ready to begin using the system.

### Accessing your project[¶](#accessing-your-project "Permalink to this headline")

Once you recieve your Dawn username and Slurm project name, please go to projects and you can see projects that you were invited, please click on project and after that select the project that you want to access.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_user_projects.png)](../_images/waldur_user_projects.png)

Upon opening your project, kindly navigate to the Resources tab to review the available resources that you can access.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_user_resources.png)](../_images/waldur_user_resources.png)

On the main page, you will find the URL to access the system. Please click on it and begin using the platform after completing the authentication process.
Please note that you will gain an additional MFA code, labelled “CSD3:<userid>”, where <userid> is your Dawn username. Please do not delete this code.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_user_gettingstarted.png)](../_images/waldur_user_gettingstarted.png)

Upon completing the authentication process, you will gain access to the resources.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/hpc_weblogin.png)](../_images/hpc_weblogin.png)

## First Time Accessing Dawn - SSH[¶](#first-time-accessing-dawn-ssh "Permalink to this headline")

In addition to the above web interface (login-web) you can also access Dawn via terminal.

If you are not part of the University of Cambridge, you will need to submit you ssh public key to the service desk. Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk and attach your public key for Dawn ssh access.
